summary.difficultyAssessment: D1:128 (30%), D2:159 (37%), D3:146 (34%) — close to GL target 30:40:30 but D2 is slightly under-represented and D3 slightly over. D1 questions are heavily weighted toward missing apostrophes in contractions (don't, can't, won't, etc.) — these are very repetitive, with dozens of near-identical questions. D2 adds commas, possessive apostrophes, speech marks, and its/it's. D3 covers comma splices, restrictive vs non-restrictive clauses, hyphens, and colons. The difficulty progression is sound but D1 variety is poor.
summary.coverageAssessment: Good breadth across GL-tested punctuation categories: apostrophes (contractions and possession), commas (lists, clauses, parenthetical), capital letters, speech marks, colons/semicolons, brackets, hyphens, question marks. However, the distribution is heavily skewed — estimated 40%+ of all questions test missing apostrophes in contractions, which is disproportionate. Capital letter questions are also over-represented at D1. Under-represented: apostrophe in plural possession (dogs' vs dog's), ellipsis, dash usage, and advanced semicolon usage. No questions test the apostrophe in time expressions (two weeks' holiday) or abbreviations.
summary.teachingEfficacyAssessment: 28 lessons across 14 sub-concepts provide comprehensive coverage. Lessons are warm, age-appropriate, and use memorable analogies (apostrophes as 'flags saying letters were here'). The 'it's vs its' lessons with the 'swap in it is' trick are particularly well designed. Variable sets (3 per lesson) ensure variety. However, multiple explanations contain a specific bug pattern where the correction repeats the error in lowercase rather than showing the corrected version. All 433 questions are mapped (0 unmapped) with 431 high-confidence mappings. 1 orphan sub-concept exists. Teaching quality is strong but some explanations need fixing.
topPriorities: Fix ~15 capital letter question explanations that say 'should be X' but show the lowercase version instead of capitalised
Fix Q7 and Q18 — marked 'No mistake' but both contain missing commas around non-defining relative clauses
Fix Q116 — marked 'No mistake' but segment D contains 'new the answer' (should be 'knew')
Fix Q200/Q202/Q204/Q206 — wrong explanation type (says 'comma after opening phrase' when the error is missing speech punctuation)
Reduce D1 repetition — too many near-identical 'missing apostrophe in contraction' questions

issues.title: Q7 marked 'No mistake' but sentence needs commas around non-defining relative clause
issues.description: Q7 segments form: 'My sister who is seven years old loves reading books about animals.' This is marked as 'No mistake' (correct=4). However, 'who is seven years old' is a non-defining relative clause — my sister is a specific, unique person, so the clause is extra information, not defining. It should be set off with commas: 'My sister, who is seven years old, loves reading books about animals.' The answer should be Segment A (missing comma after 'sister') or Segment B (missing comma after 'old').
issues.suggestedFix: Either change the correct answer to indicate where commas are needed, or rephrase to use a defining context (e.g. 'The girl who is seven years old loves reading') where commas are genuinely not needed.

issues.title: Q18 marked 'No mistake' but sentence needs commas around non-defining relative clause
issues.description: Q18 segments form: 'My grandparents who live in Scotland are coming to visit us next week.' Marked as 'No mistake'. However, 'who live in Scotland' is non-defining — if you only have one set of grandparents, the clause is extra information and needs commas. At minimum this is ambiguous, and at D2 level this is exactly the kind of question GL uses to test understanding of parenthetical commas.
issues.suggestedFix: Either change the correct answer to flag the missing commas, or change the subject to make the clause genuinely defining (e.g. 'The neighbours who live next door are coming to visit' where 'who live next door' distinguishes these neighbours from others).

issues.title: Q116 marked 'No mistake' but segment D contains 'new' instead of 'knew'
issues.description: Q116 segments: 'The teacher asked / the class a question / but nobody / new the answer.' This is marked 'No mistake' (correct=4) with explanation 'The apostrophe is in exactly the right place.' However, 'new the answer' is clearly wrong — it should be 'knew the answer'. While this is technically a spelling/homophone error rather than punctuation, the question claims no mistake exists. The explanation about apostrophes is also irrelevant since no apostrophe appears in the sentence.
issues.suggestedFix: Change the correct answer to Segment D and update the explanation: "'new' should be 'knew'. 'Knew' is the past tense of 'know', while 'new' means 'not old'. These homophones sound the same but mean completely different things."

issues.title: Capital letter explanations show lowercase correction instead of capitalised version
issues.description: Approximately 20 capital letter questions have explanations that say the word 'should be' followed by the lowercase version. For example, Q12: "'paris' needs a capital letter — it should be 'paris'" (should say 'Paris'). Q27: "'saturday' needs a capital letter — it should be 'saturday'" (should say 'Saturday'). Q58: "'edinburgh' needs a capital letter — it should be 'edinburgh'" (should say 'Edinburgh'). The correct answer in all cases IS correct — it's only the explanation text that fails to show the capitalised form. This is a systematic template bug where the explanation inserts the original error rather than the correction.
issues.suggestedFix: Fix the explanation template so that the 'should be' text shows the capitalised form. For each affected question, change the explanation to show the word with a capital letter (e.g. 'Paris' not 'paris').

issues.title: Q2 and Q13 have wrong explanation for the type of error present
issues.description: Q2 segments: 'James said / "I really enjoyed / the film we watched / yesterday."' — the error is in Segment A: there should be a comma after 'said' before the opening speech marks. But the explanation says 'A comma is needed after the opening part of the sentence' (about fronted adverbials), which is the wrong rule. Similarly Q13 segments: '"I love swimming" / said Amara / "because it makes / me feel so free."' — the error is missing comma after 'swimming' before closing speech mark, but gets the same fronted adverbial explanation. Both are actually speech punctuation errors.
issues.suggestedFix: Q2: Change explanation to: 'A comma is needed after "said" before the speech marks: James said, "I really enjoyed..." When introducing direct speech with a reporting verb, always put a comma before the opening speech marks.' Q13: Change explanation to: 'A comma is needed after "swimming" inside the speech marks: "I love swimming," said Amara. When speech comes before the reporting verb, it ends with a comma (not a full stop) inside the speech marks.'

issues.title: Speech punctuation errors given wrong 'fronted adverbial' explanation
issues.description: Q200 ('Gran exclaimed / "What a lovely...'), Q202 ('The headteacher replied / "The school fair..."), Q204 ('The librarian asked / "Has anyone seen...'), Q206 ('Dad announced / "We're going on...'): All have the error in Segment A — missing comma after the reporting verb before speech marks. But all are given the explanation 'A comma is needed after the opening part of the sentence' which describes fronted adverbials, not speech punctuation. The correct rule is that a comma is needed between the reporting clause and the direct speech.
issues.suggestedFix: Change explanations to describe the speech punctuation rule: 'A comma is needed after the reporting verb before the opening speech marks. We write: Gran exclaimed, "What a lovely surprise!" The comma tells the reader that speech is about to begin.'

issues.title: Q10 explanation describes commas in lists but the actual error is a misused semicolon
issues.description: Q10 segments: 'There are two things / I enjoy most; / swimming in the sea / and climbing mountains.' The semicolon after 'most' is incorrect — a colon should be used to introduce a list. But the explanation says 'Commas are needed to separate the items in this list' which is about list comma separation, not about the colon vs semicolon distinction. The actual punctuation error is the misuse of the semicolon.
issues.suggestedFix: Change explanation to: 'A colon (:) should be used here, not a semicolon (;). When you introduce a list with a statement like "the following things" or "two things", use a colon to announce the list. A semicolon joins two complete sentences — it does not introduce lists.'

issues.title: Q40 'No mistake' explanation mentions apostrophe but no apostrophe exists in sentence
issues.description: Q40 (correct=4, 'No mistake'): 'I know you have / been practising hard / for the spelling test / all this week.' Explanation says 'This one's a trick — there's actually nothing wrong here! The apostrophe is in exactly the right place.' But the sentence contains no apostrophe at all. The explanation text is a generic template that does not match this question.
issues.suggestedFix: Change explanation to: 'This one's a trick — there's actually nothing wrong here! All the punctuation is correct.'

issues.title: Q25 tests spelling/word choice (there/their), not punctuation
issues.description: Q25 segments: 'The teachers asked / the students to / complete there homework / before Monday morning.' The error is 'there' instead of 'their' — this is a homophone/spelling error, not a punctuation error. The explanation even acknowledges this: 'This is technically a spelling/grammar error but commonly tested in punctuation sections.' While GL does occasionally embed homophones in punctuation sections, having this in a punctuation-only question bank without clear labelling may confuse learners about what constitutes punctuation.
issues.suggestedFix: Consider moving this to the grammar/spelling topic, or adding a tag/note that this tests homophones rather than punctuation marks. If kept, ensure the explanation clearly distinguishes this from punctuation errors.

issues.title: Q26 explanation claims comma exists where there is none
issues.description: Q26 segments: 'The concert which / featured three local bands / was held in / the town hall.' The explanation says: 'That comma shouldn't be there! The information in this part of the sentence is essential...' But Segment A ('The concert which') does not contain a comma. If the intended error is the ABSENCE of a comma (that 'The concert, which' should have a comma because it's non-defining), then the answer and explanation are internally contradictory. If the concert is one specific concert, 'which featured three local bands' is non-defining and SHOULD have commas.
issues.suggestedFix: Clarify whether this question tests restrictive vs non-restrictive clauses. If the intent is that commas are missing (non-defining clause), change the explanation to reflect that. If the intent is that no commas are needed (defining clause), the explanation should not reference a comma that doesn't exist. Consider marking as 'No mistake' if the clause is intended as defining, or adding commas to other segments if it's intended as non-defining.

issues.title: Q78 same restrictive clause ambiguity as Q26
issues.description: Q78 segments: 'The old cottage which / had stood for centuries / was finally sold / to a young couple.' Explanation says 'That comma shouldn't be there' but no comma exists in Segment A. Same issue pattern as Q26 — the explanation references removing a comma that isn't present. Whether 'which had stood for centuries' is restrictive or non-restrictive depends on context, and at D3 this is a legitimate GL trap, but the explanation must match the actual content of the segments.
issues.suggestedFix: Either add commas around the relative clause in the segments (making the error their presence) and keep the explanation, or rewrite the explanation to address what error is actually present. If 'No mistake' is intended because this is a restrictive clause, change the answer to 4.

issues.title: Q77 says commas are needed but segments already contain commas
issues.description: Q77 segments: 'We need flour, sugar / butter, eggs and / milk to bake / the birthday cake.' Answer is Segment A, explanation says 'Commas are needed to separate the items in this list.' But Segment A already has a comma after 'flour' and Segment B has one after 'butter'. The actual error appears to be a missing comma after 'sugar' (Segment A ends with 'sugar' but no comma before 'butter' in Segment B). The explanation is misleading because it suggests no commas exist at all.
issues.suggestedFix: Update explanation to be specific: 'A comma is needed after "sugar" to separate it from "butter" in the list. The list should read: flour, sugar, butter, eggs and milk.'

issues.title: Excessive repetition of D1 missing-apostrophe-in-contraction questions
issues.description: Of 128 D1 questions, approximately 70-80 test the same pattern: a contraction missing its apostrophe (don't, can't, won't, isn't, hasn't, weren't, etc.). Many use nearly identical sentence structures and the same boilerplate explanation ('missing its apostrophe... think of it as a little flag saying letters were here!'). While apostrophe contractions are important, this level of repetition provides diminishing returns and crowds out other D1 punctuation concepts (comma in simple list, question mark vs full stop, capital letter at start of sentence).
issues.suggestedFix: Reduce contraction apostrophe questions at D1 to ~30 (from ~70+) and replace with more variety: simple comma-in-list errors, missing question marks, missing full stops, and capital letter errors at sentence start. This would better reflect the range of D1 punctuation errors GL tests.

issues.title: Heavy answer position bias in D2 question ranges
issues.description: In the Q200-220 range, at least 15 consecutive questions have the correct answer at position 0 (Segment A) or position 1 (Segment B). GL distributes errors roughly evenly across all four segments. Long runs of the same answer position allow test-wise children to spot patterns and game the answers, which is not authentic to the GL experience.
issues.suggestedFix: Redistribute the answer positions across A/B/C/D more evenly in the D2 question range. Ensure no more than 3-4 consecutive questions share the same answer position.

issues.title: Q85 explanation incorrectly uses capital letter template for start-of-sentence capital
issues.description: Q85 segments: '"Can we go to the / park after lunch?" / asked Ben. "yes, I / think we can," said Dad.' The error is 'yes' needing a capital 'Y' because it starts a new sentence of direct speech. But the explanation says 'Names of people, places, days of the week, and months always start with a capital letter' — this is the proper noun template, not the start-of-sentence template. The capital is needed because 'Yes' starts a new sentence, not because it's a proper noun.
issues.suggestedFix: Change explanation to: '"yes" needs a capital letter because it starts a new sentence of direct speech. After the reporting clause ("asked Ben"), the next piece of speech begins a new sentence and must start with a capital letter: "Yes, I think we can."'

issues.title: Q86 explanation uses proper noun template for start-of-sentence capital
issues.description: Q86 segments: '"Watch out!" cried Sam. / "That branch / is about to fall." / "thanks for warning me."' — 'thanks' needs a capital because it starts a new sentence of direct speech. Explanation incorrectly applies the proper noun rule.
issues.suggestedFix: Same fix as Q85 — use start-of-sentence rule in explanation, not the proper noun rule.

issues.title: Q84 explanation describes fronted adverbial but the error is missing comma in interrupted speech
issues.description: Q84 segments: '"Please sit down," / said Mrs Harris / "and open your / books to page ten."' — the error is in Segment B: a comma is needed after 'Harris' before resuming speech ('said Mrs Harris, "and open...'). The explanation describes fronted adverbials ('when a sentence doesn't start with the main action'). The correct rule is about punctuating interrupted direct speech.
issues.suggestedFix: Change explanation to: 'A comma is needed after "Harris" before the speech continues. When speech is interrupted by a reporting clause (said Mrs Harris), you need a comma after the reporting clause before the speech marks reopen.'

issues.title: Q62 (capital letter for 'Aunt Sarah') rated D1 but involves a nuanced capitalisation rule
issues.description: Q62 tests capitalisation of 'aunt' in 'aunt Sarah'. The rule for capitalising family titles (Aunt Sarah vs 'my aunt') is actually a D2 concept — it requires understanding that titles used as names (Aunt Sarah) get capitals, but generic references (my aunt) do not. At D1, capital letter questions should test obvious cases like days, months, and place names.
issues.suggestedFix: Reclassify as D2.

issues.title: Q74 tests debatable comma-before-'but' rule at D2
issues.description: Q74 segments: 'The bag contained / a pencil, a rubber, / a ruler and a / sharpener but no eraser.' Explanation says comma needed before 'but'. However, the clause after 'but' ('no eraser') is not an independent clause — it's a phrase. The 'comma before but' rule only applies when 'but' joins two independent clauses. This is a borderline case that could confuse learners.
issues.suggestedFix: Either reclassify as D3 (where ambiguous comma rules are appropriate) or change the sentence so 'but' clearly joins two independent clauses (e.g. 'The bag was full, but the eraser was missing.').

issues.title: Q4 (irregular plural possessive) mapped to 'Apostrophes in Contractions' instead of 'Apostrophes for Possession'
issues.description: Q4 tests 'childrens'' vs 'children's' — an irregular plural possession question. But it is mapped to sub-concept 'apostrophe-contraction' (Apostrophes in Contractions). This should be mapped to 'apostrophe-possession' (Apostrophes for Possession) since the error is about possessive apostrophes, not contractions.
issues.suggestedFix: Change mapping to sub-concept 'apostrophe-possession'.

issues.title: 'No mistake' questions may be under-represented or lack challenge
issues.description: The Oracle recommends 15-20% of punctuation questions should have 'No mistake' as the answer. Several 'No mistake' questions use simple sentences with no tricky-looking punctuation, making them too easy — the child isn't tempted to pick an error. GL's 'No mistake' questions work by including correctly-punctuated elements that look wrong (e.g. Thomas' pencil case, both teachers' classrooms) to create doubt. Some questions in this set do this well (Q47, Q49, Q52) but others (Q40, Q65, Q124) have entirely straightforward sentences.
issues.suggestedFix: Ensure 'No mistake' questions consistently include at least one element that children commonly doubt (irregular possessives, its/it's used correctly, commas that look optional). Remove 'No mistake' questions with entirely simple sentences.

issues.title: One orphan sub-concept has no questions mapped to it
issues.description: The mappings file reports 1 orphan sub-concept — a lesson sub-concept that exists but has no questions mapped to it. This means the lesson teaches something that is never tested, which is a wasted learning opportunity.
issues.suggestedFix: Identify the orphan sub-concept and either create questions for it or merge its content into an existing sub-concept that does have questions.
